title: Representation-Induced Symmetry Trapping in Adaptive Variational Quantum Simulations of Multi-Reference Topologies
abstract: Evaluating the trainability of adaptive quantum chemistry algorithms under multi-reference static correlation requires understanding how representation topologies intertwine with molecular geometry. We systematically expose a deep physical dependence on point-group symmetry by evaluating a spin-conserved SUSD operator pool across highly stretched configurations (2 x Re) of asymmetric LiH, symmetric BeH2, and asymmetric H2O. Under asymmetric distortions, the non-local mapping constraints of the Bravyi-Kitaev transformation create an optimization trapping effect--an encodement-locked manifestation of the broader barren plateau crisis. Crucially, by comparing these to the symmetrical stretching baseline of BeH2, we demonstrate that the preservation of point-group symmetry structurally protects the optimization landscape, proving that ansatz symmetry restrictions are necessary but insufficient without accounting for the underlying fermion-to-qubit representation. While current methods rely on numerical pruning to throttle pool sizes, our structural approach establishes that the mapping representation remains a critical factor in maintaining landscape trainability. Furthermore, exploiting structural overlap within our pool, we introduce a covariance-driven, adaptive shot-allocation filter. Diverging from static energy-variance minimization frameworks, our allocation engine operates as a dynamic runtime diagnostic tool. By continuously monitoring the gradient precision threshold epsilon, it aggressively prunes dead symmetry channels and triggers an automated circuit-termination sequence upon detecting representation-induced flat-lined states (dE/dtheta approx 0). This integration of algebraic measurement reuse with topology-aware statistical filtering provides a promising, resource-efficient strategy for executing deep variational algorithms on early fault-tolerant architectures.
summary: This paper investigates how mapping fermions to qubits affects the feasibility of running variational quantum simulations for molecular electronic structure. The authors find that certain mappings create artificial 'traps' in the optimization landscape, especially under asymmetric stretching. They propose a novel, adaptive filtering technique that diagnoses these topological failures by monitoring gradient stability, offering a resource-efficient path toward fault-tolerant quantum chemistry.
main_problem: The study evaluates how representation topologies, specifically arising from fermionic-to-qubit mappings (JW vs. BK), impact the trainability of adaptive quantum chemistry algorithms when simulating multi-reference electronic structures.
main_result: Asymmetric molecular distortions cause the Bravyi-Kitaev mapping to induce an optimization trapping effect, while preserving point-group symmetry structurally protects the landscape; a dynamic shot-allocation filter can mitigate these traps.
method: The authors use ADAPT-VQE on stretched molecules and introduce a covariance-driven adaptive shot-allocation filter that monitors gradient precision ($\epsilon$) to diagnose and halt computation upon detecting representation-induced flat-lined states.
model_or_system: The simulation models molecular electronic structure using the Hamiltonian mapped into an active space, tested on highly stretched configurations of LiH, BeH2, and H2O. The core computational challenge involves mapping fermionic operators to qubits via JW or BK transformations.
key_observables: Optimization landscape trainability, energy convergence status (Clean Convergence vs. Gradient Stall), gradient precision ($\epsilon$), and Pauli Term count.
important_parameters: Highly stretched geometries ($2 	imes Re$) of LiH, BeH2, H2O; the adaptive threshold $\epsilon$; and the choice between JW and BK mappings.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes that mapping representation is a critical factor in maintaining landscape trainability, and it proposes an adaptive filter to manage resource allocation based on gradient diagnostics.
figures_summary: Table 1 compares performance metrics (Pauli Terms, Energy, Depth, Status) across different systems and mappers. Table 2 lists nuclear coordinates for the benchmark molecules. Appendix A contains telemetry logs showing convergence behavior.
paper_structure: The paper systematically analyzes ADAPT-VQE on stretched molecules, comparing JW and BK mappings to identify topological trapping effects. It then introduces a novel adaptive shot-allocation filter that diagnoses these traps by monitoring gradient precision ($\epsilon$) and triggering termination when symmetry constraints cause stagnation.
why_interesting: This work directly addresses the practical limitations of applying quantum algorithms to complex chemistry problems, providing a diagnostic tool (the adaptive filter) that moves beyond simple resource counting to diagnose fundamental topological failures in the optimization landscape itself.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
